fact: Four hundred lumber mills operating in Minnesota during this era
source: Hinckley Fire Museum / Minnesota Historical Society records; Daniel James Brown, Under a Flaming Sky (2006)

fact: Drought conditions turned logging slash (debris) into kindling before the fire
source: Daniel James Brown, Under a Flaming Sky: The Great Hinckley Firestorm of 1894 (2006)

fact: Small fires smoldered for weeks before the main firestorm, and burning slash was standard practice for clearing land
source: Grace Stageberg Swenson, From the Ashes: The Story of the Hinckley Fire of 1894 (1979); widely documented in Minnesota Historical Society records

fact: Fires merged and created hurricane-force winds as superheated air drew in oxygen (firestorm convection dynamics)
source: Daniel James Brown, Under a Flaming Sky (2006); the fire generated a firestorm with winds estimated at 60+ mph creating its own weather system

fact: Trees exploding before flames reached them due to superheated gases and radiant heat
source: This phenomenon (pyrolysis and gas ignition ahead of the flame front) is documented in firestorm accounts; described in Brown, Under a Flaming Sky (2006) and consistent with wildfire science

fact: The town of Hinckley was essentially destroyed by the fire in 1894
source: Minnesota Historical Society — Hinckley Fire, September 1, 1894; over 400 people killed and multiple towns destroyed
